texts.text: A.1.2 Powersets

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We write P ( Z ) for the powerset of Z , and we define P + ( Z ) := P ( Z ) \ {∅} .
texts.text: We write P ( Z ) for the powerset of Z , and we define P + ( Z ) := P ( Z ) \ {∅} .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A.1.3 Tuples and Matrices
texts.text: A.1.3 Tuples and Matrices

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For n ∈ N , the set of Z -valued n -vectors , or n -tuples over Z , is Z n := {⟨ z 1 , . . . , z n ⟩ | z i ∈ Z } . We typically write an element of Z n as z = ⟨ z 1 , . . . , z n ⟩ . For m,n ∈ N , the set of Z -valued ( m × n ) -vectors , or m × n matrices over Z , is Z m × n := {⟨ z 1 , . . . , z m ⟩ | z i ∈ Z n } . We typically write an element of Z m × n as Z = ⟨ z 1 , . . . , z n ⟩ .
texts.text: For n ∈ N , the set of Z -valued n -vectors , or n -tuples over Z , is Z n := {⟨ z 1 , . . . , z n ⟩ | z i ∈ Z } . We typically write an element of Z n as z = ⟨ z 1 , . . . , z n ⟩ . For m,n ∈ N , the set of Z -valued ( m × n ) -vectors , or m × n matrices over Z , is Z m × n := {⟨ z 1 , . . . , z m ⟩ | z i ∈ Z n } . We typically write an element of Z m × n as Z = ⟨ z 1 , . . . , z n ⟩ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We use the compact notation Z [ i..j ] to specify the set Z i ×··· × Z j resulting from the Cartesian product of the sets Z i , . . . , Z j , meaning that they are contextually introduced by the notation.
texts.text: We use the compact notation Z [ i..j ] to specify the set Z i ×··· × Z j resulting from the Cartesian product of the sets Z i , . . . , Z j , meaning that they are contextually introduced by the notation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A.1.4 Sequences
texts.text: A.1.4 Sequences

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A sequence over Z with indices I ⊆ N is a function s : I → Z ′ ⊆ Z ,which we commonly present as ( z i ) i ∈ I where z i = s ( i ) for every i ∈ I . A sequence is finite if so is its index set, and it is infinite otherwise. When s is an infinite sequence with index set of the form I = { m, m +1 , . . . } , we adopt a simplified notation and write the sequence as ( z i ) i ≥ m , instead of ( z i ) i ∈ I . When s is a finite sequence, the cardinality of its index set is called the length of s . The empty sequence , denoted by ε , is the sequence having length zero, i.e., the sequence with indices I = ∅ . Any finite sequence s with indices I = [ i..j ] can be presented as the list z i , . . . , z j by letting z k = s ( k ) for every k ∈ [ i..j ] ; in this case, the sequence can also be written in compact form as z [ i..j ] . We write Z ω for the set of all infinite sequences on Z , we write Z ∗ for the set of all finite sequences on Z , we write Z + for the set of all non-empty finite sequences on Z , and we write Z ℓ for the set of all sequence of a given length ℓ ∈ N -noting that this definition of Z ℓ clearly corresponds to the definition given above of Z ℓ as the set of all ℓ -tuples over Z .
texts.text: A sequence over Z with indices I ⊆ N is a function s : I → Z ′ ⊆ Z ,which we commonly present as ( z i ) i ∈ I where z i = s ( i ) for every i ∈ I . A sequence is finite if so is its index set, and it is infinite otherwise. When s is an infinite sequence with index set of the form I = { m, m +1 , . . . } , we adopt a simplified notation and write the sequence as ( z i ) i ≥ m , instead of ( z i ) i ∈ I . When s is a finite sequence, the cardinality of its index set is called the length of s . The empty sequence , denoted by ε , is the sequence having length zero, i.e., the sequence with indices I = ∅ . Any finite sequence s with indices I = [ i..j ] can be presented as the list z i , . . . , z j by letting z k = s ( k ) for every k ∈ [ i..j ] ; in this case, the sequence can also be written in compact form as z [ i..j ] . We write Z ω for the set of all infinite sequences on Z , we write Z ∗ for the set of all finite sequences on Z , we write Z + for the set of all non-empty finite sequences on Z , and we write Z ℓ for the set of all sequence of a given length ℓ ∈ N -noting that this definition of Z ℓ clearly corresponds to the definition given above of Z ℓ as the set of all ℓ -tuples over Z .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We often say that a property holds eventually for a sequence ( z i ) i ≥ m if there exists m ′ ≥ m such that it holds for the sequence ( z i ) i ≥ m ′ . That is, the property holds for some tail of the sequence.
texts.text: We often say that a property holds eventually for a sequence ( z i ) i ≥ m if there exists m ′ ≥ m such that it holds for the sequence ( z i ) i ≥ m ′ . That is, the property holds for some tail of the sequence.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A.1.5 Strings
texts.text: A.1.5 Strings

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A string over a finite set Σ is a concatenation (juxtaposition) of elements of Σ . Namely, a string is an expression σ 1 σ 2 · · · σ n with σ i ∈ Σ , for every i ∈ [1 ..n ] . In this context, we call Σ an alphabet , and we call each element σ i a letter or symbol of the string s . We can equivalently see a string σ 1 σ 2 · · · σ n as the finite sequence σ [1 ..n ] , following the definition of finite sequence given above, and hence apply all notions already introduced for finite sequences. In particular, we have that the length of a string σ 1 σ 2 · · · σ n is n , that ε is the empty string, that Σ ℓ is the set of all strings of given length ℓ ∈ N over alphabet Σ , that Σ ∗ is the set of all strings over alphabet Σ , and that Σ + is the set of all non-empty strings over alphabet Σ .
texts.text: A string over a finite set Σ is a concatenation (juxtaposition) of elements of Σ . Namely, a string is an expression σ 1 σ 2 · · · σ n with σ i ∈ Σ , for every i ∈ [1 ..n ] . In this context, we call Σ an alphabet , and we call each element σ i a letter or symbol of the string s . We can equivalently see a string σ 1 σ 2 · · · σ n as the finite sequence σ [1 ..n ] , following the definition of finite sequence given above, and hence apply all notions already introduced for finite sequences. In particular, we have that the length of a string σ 1 σ 2 · · · σ n is n , that ε is the empty string, that Σ ℓ is the set of all strings of given length ℓ ∈ N over alphabet Σ , that Σ ∗ is the set of all strings over alphabet Σ , and that Σ + is the set of all non-empty strings over alphabet Σ .

texts.orig: A.1.6 Functions and Transformations
texts.text: A.1.6 Functions and Transformations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The image of a function f : X → Y is Im f := { f ( x ) | x ∈ X } ⊆ Y . We say that f is an identity if f ( x ) = x for every x ∈ X , and we say that f is a permutation if it is a bijection. A transformation of X is a function f : X → X where the codomain coincides with the domain. Note that every identity transformation is also a permutation, and hence it is sometimes important to distinguish permutations that are not identities by referring to them as non-identity permutations .

texts.text: A.2.2 Metric spaces

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A metric space is a tuple S = ⟨ X,d ⟩ where d : X × X → R is a metric. Given metric spaces X = ⟨ X,d X ⟩ and Y = ⟨ Y, d Y ⟩ , an isometry between X and Y (or distance-preserving function ) is a bijective function f : X → Y such that, for every 1 , x 2 ∈ X , we have d X ( x 1 , x 2 ) = d Y ( f ( x 1 ) , f ( x 2 )) . When an isometry exists, the spaces X and Y are said to be isometric . Intuitively, two isometric spaces are essentially the same metric space. Notable metric spaces, relevant to us, are the following ones, for n ∈ N > 0 .

When an isometry exists, the spaces X and Y are said to be isometric . Intuitively, two isometric spaces are essentially the same metric space. texts.orig: We define the open ball B X ( x, r ) and closed ball B X ( x, r ) at x ∈ X of radius r ≥ 0 in ⟨ X,d ⟩ as the set of points in X with distance δ < r and δ ≤ r from x , respectively:
texts.text: We define the open ball B X ( x, r ) and closed ball B X ( x, r ) at x ∈ X of radius r ≥ 0 in ⟨ X,d ⟩ as the set of points in X with distance δ < r and δ ≤ r from x , respectively:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: B X ( x, r ) := { y ∈ X | d ( x, y ) < r } , B X ( x, r ) := { y ∈ X | d ( x, y ) ≤ r } .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A subspace ( Y, d Y ) of ( X,d X ) is a metric space with Y ⊆ X and d Y given by restriction of d X to Y × Y . We say that a subspace S ⊆ X is bounded , if there is some x ∈ X and ∞ > M ≥ 0 s.t. S ⊆ B X ( x, M ) . We call a subspace S ⊆ X is open in X if for all s ∈ S there is some ϵ s > 0 s.t. B X ( s, ϵ s ) ⊆ S . S is closed in X if X \ S is open in X .
texts.text: A subspace ( Y, d Y ) of ( X,d X ) is a metric space with Y ⊆ X and d Y given by restriction of d X to Y × Y . We say that a subspace S ⊆ X is bounded , if there is some x ∈ X and ∞ > M ≥ 0 s.t. S ⊆ B X ( x, M ) . We call a subspace S ⊆ X is open in X if for all s ∈ S there is some ϵ s > 0 s.t. B X ( s, ϵ s ) ⊆ S . S is closed in X if X \ S is open in X .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A.2.3 Topology
texts.text: A.2.3 Topology

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The notion of open subspaces in terms of open balls defines a topology on any metric space, which determines what functions are continuous . Formally, a topological space is a tuple ( S, T ) , with S being the underlying set, and T ⊆ P ( S ) being the collection of open sets, such that S and ∅ , the union of any collection of open sets is open, and the intersection of any finite collection of open sets is open. The open sets definition in terms of open balls for a metric space satisfies these properties. Many aspects of Metric Automata Theory could be easily restated in the language of Topology Theory, but we choose a more concrete setting, to make it more accessible.
texts.text: The notion of open subspaces in terms of open balls defines a topology on any metric space, which determines what functions are continuous . Formally, a topological space is a tuple ( S, T ) , with S being the underlying set, and T ⊆ P ( S ) being the collection of open sets, such that S and ∅ , the union of any collection of open sets is open, and the intersection of any finite collection of open sets is open. The open sets definition in terms of open balls for a metric space satisfies these properties. Many aspects of Metric Automata Theory could be easily restated in the language of Topology Theory, but we choose a more concrete setting, to make it more accessible.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Intuitively, the closed subspaces of X are precisely the ones which contain all their limit points, i.e. if ( x n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ S converges to some limit l ∈ X , then l ∈ S .
texts.text: Intuitively, the closed subspaces of X are precisely the ones which contain all their limit points, i.e. if ( x n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ S converges to some limit l ∈ X , then l ∈ S .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fact A.2.1. For a metric space X , a subset S ⊆ X is closed iff for all sequences ( x n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ S converging to l ∈ X we have that l ∈ S . (see §10, Cor. 10.5 of Willard [2012], as every metric space is first-countable)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that the notion of opennes/closeness is not inherent to the subspace S : it also depends on the superspace X , since the definition involves balls in X . In fact, any subspace S ⊆ X is by definition both open and closed as a subspace of itself, regardless of whether is open or closed in X . Any time we use opennes or open balls, we need to excercise caution and be clear which space the openness is referring to.

However, it is not even an open set in M ! For any ϵ > 0 we have || (0 , 0) -(0 , ϵ ) || = ϵ , but (0 , ϵ ) / ∈ S , and so no open X -ball centred at (0 , 0) is wholly contained in S . ■

The ϵ -δ definition of continuity, as well as the topological definition of continuity ( Y ⊆ M ′ open = ⇒ f -1 ( Y ) ⊆ M open) are equivalent in the metric space setting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 9. A space X is called compact if all coverings of X by open subsets of X admit a finite subcover. For metric spaces, equivalently X is (sequentially) compact, if all sequences in X have a subsequence converging to a limit in X (see 17G.3 of Willard [2012]). ■
texts.text: Definition 9. A space X is called compact if all coverings of X by open subsets of X admit a finite subcover. For metric spaces, equivalently X is (sequentially) compact, if all sequences in X have a subsequence converging to a limit in X (see 17G.3 of Willard [2012]). ■

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A.2.5 Path-connectedness
texts.text: A.2.5 Path-connectedness

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 10. A path in X from a to b is a continuous function γ : [0 , 1] → X such that γ (0) = a and γ (1) = b . A space X is called path-connected if for all a, b ∈ X there is a path from a to b . ■
texts.text: Definition 10. A path in X from a to b is a continuous function γ : [0 , 1] → X such that γ (0) = a and γ (1) = b . A space X is called path-connected if for all a, b ∈ X there is a path from a to b . ■

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Path-connectedness partitions the space into components, which we will later think of as atomic parts of the state-space for a dynamical system. - any continuous decoder assigning discrete symbols to the state-space must be constant on a path-connected component, see Lemma 22.
texts.text: Path-connectedness partitions the space into components, which we will later think of as atomic parts of the state-space for a dynamical system. - any continuous decoder assigning discrete symbols to the state-space must be constant on a path-connected component, see Lemma 22.

texts.text: Fact A.2.6. The relation ∼ on X given by a ∼ b ⇐⇒ there is a path from a to b in X is an equivalence. The equivalence classes of ∼ are the maximal path-connected subspaces of X .

texts.orig: Example 5 . Any convex subspace of R d is path-connected, in particular open and closed R d -balls are path-connected. ( -1 , 0) ∪ (0 , 1) has 2 path-connected components: ( -1 , 0) and (0 , 1) . ■
texts.text: Example 5 . Any convex subspace of R d is path-connected, in particular open and closed R d -balls are path-connected. ( -1 , 0) ∪ (0 , 1) has 2 path-connected components: ( -1 , 0) and (0 , 1) . ■

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A.3 Dynamical Systems
texts.text: A.3 Dynamical Systems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Following Knorozova and Ronca [2024a], we adopt dynamical systems as an general formalism to describe all systems that operate by maintaining a state recurrently. This allows for treating such systems in a uniform way despite their differences. In this work specifically, we will use dynamical systems to formalise Finite Automata and several RNN architectures in Section A.6.
texts.text: Following Knorozova and Ronca [2024a], we adopt dynamical systems as an general formalism to describe all systems that operate by maintaining a state recurrently. This allows for treating such systems in a uniform way despite their differences. In this work specifically, we will use dynamical systems to formalise Finite Automata and several RNN architectures in Section A.6.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 11. A (dynamical) system is a tuple S = ⟨ X,U,f,x 0 , Y, h ⟩ , where X is the state space , U is the input space , f : X × U → X is the dynamics function , x 0 ∈ X is the initial state , Y is the output space and h : X × U → Y is the output function. We have that X,U,Y are metric spaces, and f, h are continuous . In our analysis it will be useful to refer to the tuple D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ as the dynamics of S , allowing us to focus on just the state transitions.
texts.text: Definition 11. A (dynamical) system is a tuple S = ⟨ X,U,f,x 0 , Y, h ⟩ , where X is the state space , U is the input space , f : X × U → X is the dynamics function , x 0 ∈ X is the initial state , Y is the output space and h : X × U → Y is the output function. We have that X,U,Y are metric spaces, and f, h are continuous . In our analysis it will be useful to refer to the tuple D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ as the dynamics of S , allowing us to focus on just the state transitions.

texts.orig: The formalism of cascades provides a flexible way to describe dynamical systems consisting of subsystems forming an acyclic network. Their flexibility will allows us, e.g., to consider not only feed-forward layers of SSMs as in Grazzi et al. [2025], Sarrof et al. [2024], but also more complex architectures with, e.g., blocks in parallel, and mixes of different types of neurons.
texts.text: The formalism of cascades provides a flexible way to describe dynamical systems consisting of subsystems forming an acyclic network. Their flexibility will allows us, e.g., to consider not only feed-forward layers of SSMs as in Grazzi et al. [2025], Sarrof et al. [2024], but also more complex architectures with, e.g., blocks in parallel, and mixes of different types of neurons.

texts.orig: Thus, the cascade is evaluated in a feedforward fashion: on input u , first the state of D 1 is updated, then for all subsequent components D i , the state of D i is updated based on u and the updated states of D 1 , . . . , D i -1 . This differs from some recurrent neural network literature, where D i is updated based on u and the initial states of D 1 , . . . , D i -1 , i.e. the update happens at the same time for all components. We refer to such cascades as serial cascades .

This differs from some recurrent neural network literature, where D i is updated based on u and the initial states of D 1 , . . . , D i -1 , i.e. the update happens at the same time for all components. We refer to such cascades as serial cascades .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A.4 Algebraic Automata Theory (AAT)
texts.text: A.4 Algebraic Automata Theory (AAT)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We present an extended version of the background on Algebraic Automata Theory given in the preliminaries of the main body.
texts.text: We present an extended version of the background on Algebraic Automata Theory given in the preliminaries of the main body.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Algebraic Automata Theory (AAT) allows for studying finite automata through the lens of algebraic notions such as semigroups and groups, c.f. [Hartmanis and Stearns, 1966, Ginzburg, 1968, Arbib, 1969, Dömösi and Nehaniv, 2005]. Its fundamental theorem is the seminal Prime Decomposition Theorem by Krohn and Rhodes [1965], that shows how every semiautomaton can be decomposed into a cascade of elementary prime semiautomata. One prime semiautomaton is the flip-flop , that describes the elementary system with the ability to store and manipulate one bit of information.
texts.text: Algebraic Automata Theory (AAT) allows for studying finite automata through the lens of algebraic notions such as semigroups and groups, c.f. [Hartmanis and Stearns, 1966, Ginzburg, 1968, Arbib, 1969, Dömösi and Nehaniv, 2005]. Its fundamental theorem is the seminal Prime Decomposition Theorem by Krohn and Rhodes [1965], that shows how every semiautomaton can be decomposed into a cascade of elementary prime semiautomata. One prime semiautomaton is the flip-flop , that describes the elementary system with the ability to store and manipulate one bit of information.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: AAT often focuses on state transformations rather than on the transition function δ of an automaton. State transformations are the functions δ σ ( q ) := δ ( q, σ ) obtained by fixing an input σ . They allow us to characterise semiautomata in terms of semigroups and groups. In particular, the transitive closure of the state transformations of an automaton forms a semigroup, and a monoid or group in special
texts.text: AAT often focuses on state transformations rather than on the transition function δ of an automaton. State transformations are the functions δ σ ( q ) := δ ( q, σ ) obtained by fixing an input σ . They allow us to characterise semiautomata in terms of semigroups and groups. In particular, the transitive closure of the state transformations of an automaton forms a semigroup, and a monoid or group in special

texts.label: text
texts.orig: cases. From this algebraic point of view, the flip-flop is characterised by the flip-flop semigroup, which is in fact given by the set of state transformations of FLIP-FLOP. All the other primes are characterised by finite simple groups, and for this reason they are called group-like . Specifically, their state transformations form a finite simple group.
texts.text: cases. From this algebraic point of view, the flip-flop is characterised by the flip-flop semigroup, which is in fact given by the set of state transformations of FLIP-FLOP. All the other primes are characterised by finite simple groups, and for this reason they are called group-like . Specifically, their state transformations form a finite simple group.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Automata whose semiautomaton can be decomposed purely into flip-flops are called group-free , and they play a central role in our theory and in general, due to the following theorem whose proof also involves the celebrated theorem by Schützenberger [1965]) on aperiodic semiautomata, cf. [Ginzburg, 1968].
texts.text: Automata whose semiautomaton can be decomposed purely into flip-flops are called group-free , and they play a central role in our theory and in general, due to the following theorem whose proof also involves the celebrated theorem by Schützenberger [1965]) on aperiodic semiautomata, cf. [Ginzburg, 1968].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 12. The star-free languages is the class of languages recognised by groupfree automata.
texts.text: Theorem 12. The star-free languages is the class of languages recognised by groupfree automata.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All other automata, that do not admit the above decomposition, are called non-group-free , since their prime decompositions always include group-like semiautomata. They admit the following characterisation in terms of state transformations, relevant to our results.
texts.text: All other automata, that do not admit the above decomposition, are called non-group-free , since their prime decompositions always include group-like semiautomata. They admit the following characterisation in terms of state transformations, relevant to our results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 13. (Lemma 9 of [Knorozova and Ronca, 2024a] 1 ) If a semiautomaton ⟨ Q, Σ , δ ⟩ is not group-free, then there exist Q ′ ⊆ Q and σ ∈ Σ such that the state transformation δ σ : Q → Q is a non-identity permutation on Q ′ .
texts.text: Theorem 13. (Lemma 9 of [Knorozova and Ronca, 2024a] 1 ) If a semiautomaton ⟨ Q, Σ , δ ⟩ is not group-free, then there exist Q ′ ⊆ Q and σ ∈ Σ such that the state transformation δ σ : Q → Q is a non-identity permutation on Q ′ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our theory will extend the applicability of AAT to the study of general dynamical systems. And in particular to analyse the structure of such systems using algebraic means like group theory. A notion from AAT that is key to our results is the notion of realisation for Mealy machines (cf. Definitions 1.14 and 1.15 of [Hartmanis and Stearns, 1966]).
texts.text: Our theory will extend the applicability of AAT to the study of general dynamical systems. And in particular to analyse the structure of such systems using algebraic means like group theory. A notion from AAT that is key to our results is the notion of realisation for Mealy machines (cf. Definitions 1.14 and 1.15 of [Hartmanis and Stearns, 1966]).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Realisation describes how a machine can imitate another machine after a renaming of inputs and outputs-noting that actual names of inputs and outputs are not important in order to characterise what functionalities a machine is fundamentally able to implement.

Any trajectory through M factors through M ′ , with ι and ζ acting as the encoder and decoder, respectively, and with α providing an initial state to start from.

texts.text: A Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) is a tuple

texts.label: formula

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where d ∈ N > 0 is called the depth or number of layers , n = ⟨ n, n 2 , n 3 , . . . , n d , m ⟩ is called architecture , U ⊆ R n is the input domain, Y ⊆ R m is the output domain (or codomain), α : R → R is called activation function , β : R → R is called activation function of the last layer , W = ⟨ W 1 , . . . , W d ⟩ with W i ∈ R n i × n i +1 called weight matrices , and b = ⟨ b 1 , . . . , b d ⟩ with b i ∈ R m i called bias vectors . Then, N defines the function f : U ⊆ R n → U ⊆ R m given by the composition f 1 ◦ · · · ◦ f d of the functions f i : R n i → R n i +1 defined as
texts.text: where d ∈ N > 0 is called the depth or number of layers , n = ⟨ n, n 2 , n 3 , . . . , n d , m ⟩ is called architecture , U ⊆ R n is the input domain, Y ⊆ R m is the output domain (or codomain), α : R → R is called activation function , β : R → R is called activation function of the last layer , W = ⟨ W 1 , . . . , W d ⟩ with W i ∈ R n i × n i +1 called weight matrices , and b = ⟨ b 1 , . . . , b d ⟩ with b i ∈ R m i called bias vectors . Then, N defines the function f : U ⊆ R n → U ⊆ R m given by the composition f 1 ◦ · · · ◦ f d of the functions f i : R n i → R n i +1 defined as

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: f i ( x ) = α ( W T i x + b i ) ∀ i ∈ [1 ..d -1] , f d ( x ) = β ( W T d x + b d ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We often identify N with the function f , and hence see the network as a function N : U → Y . The functions f i are called layers , with the first layer f 1 called the input layer , the last layer f d called the output layer , and the other layers called hidden layers . The (maximum) width of N is max { n 2 , . . . , n d } . Typical choices for the activation function α are sigmoid( x ) := 1 1+exp( -x ) and the Rectified Linear Unit ReLU( x ) := max { 0 , x } . The same choices are valid for the last-layer activation function β ; however, as it computes the output of the network, it is often specialised by choosing β to be: the identity function (e.g., for regression tasks), sigmoid (e.g., for binary classification), softmax (e.g., for modelling distributions).
texts.text: We often identify N with the function f , and hence see the network as a function N : U → Y . The functions f i are called layers , with the first layer f 1 called the input layer , the last layer f d called the output layer , and the other layers called hidden layers . The (maximum) width of N is max { n 2 , . . . , n d } . Typical choices for the activation function α are sigmoid( x ) := 1 1+exp( -x ) and the Rectified Linear Unit ReLU( x ) := max { 0 , x } . The same choices are valid for the last-layer activation function β ; however, as it computes the output of the network, it is often specialised by choosing β to be: the identity function (e.g., for regression tasks), sigmoid (e.g., for binary classification), softmax (e.g., for modelling distributions).

Most generally, we can model such RNNs as acyclic networks, and for nodes N 1 , . . . , N L consider the connection functions ψ i,j , describing the transformation which is applied to the value going from neuron N i to neuron N j . The network input also may be given to N i , after going through some transformation ι i . As the network is acyclic, we may assume that there are no connection functions ψ i,j for i > j . texts.orig: xLSTM. The recently introduced model xLSTM [Beck et al., 2024] is a successor of the LSTM architecture [Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997], and it achieves performance competitive with transformer architectures. It makes use of both non-linear and linear recurrences. xLSTM introduces two types of blocks: sLSTM and mLSTM. In this work we will focus on the sLSTM block.

texts.orig: B.1 The Notion of η -Finiteness
texts.text: B.1 The Notion of η -Finiteness

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We begin by introducing η -finiteness , which is a central notion of Metric Automata Theory and our novel finite-precision framework.
texts.text: We begin by introducing η -finiteness , which is a central notion of Metric Automata Theory and our novel finite-precision framework.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 19. Let X ⊆ Ω for some d ≥ 1 . Call X η -finite if it is a finite union of compact, path-connected sets.
texts.text: Definition 19. Let X ⊆ Ω for some d ≥ 1 . Call X η -finite if it is a finite union of compact, path-connected sets.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Immediately from the definition we have that an η -finite space is necessarily compact -in the case of metric spaces, finite union of bounded, closed sets is bounded and closed. The next result resolves the technicality, that the defining sets in the union of a η -finite X need not be disjoint.
texts.text: Immediately from the definition we have that an η -finite space is necessarily compact -in the case of metric spaces, finite union of bounded, closed sets is bounded and closed. The next result resolves the technicality, that the defining sets in the union of a η -finite X need not be disjoint.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma17. Let X be η -finite. Then X has finitely many path connected components, say X 1 , . . . , X n , and each of X i is compact. We shall refer to them as the η -components of X .
texts.text: Lemma17. Let X be η -finite. Then X has finitely many path connected components, say X 1 , . . . , X n , and each of X i is compact. We shall refer to them as the η -components of X .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. By def, X = ⋃ N i =1 Y i for some compact and path-connected subsets. By induction on N : If N = 1 , then the claim is immediate. Now, consider the inductive hypothesis for N ≥ 2 , that X ′ = ⋃ N -1 i =1 Y i has finitely many path connected components X 1 , . . . , X n , each compact. The path connected components of X are then unions of elements from { X 1 , . . . , X n , Y N } . Each of these sets is compact, and so each such finite union is compact: clearly it is still bounded, and a finite union of closed sets is still closed.
texts.text: Proof. By def, X = ⋃ N i =1 Y i for some compact and path-connected subsets. By induction on N : If N = 1 , then the claim is immediate. Now, consider the inductive hypothesis for N ≥ 2 , that X ′ = ⋃ N -1 i =1 Y i has finitely many path connected components X 1 , . . . , X n , each compact. The path connected components of X are then unions of elements from { X 1 , . . . , X n , Y N } . Each of these sets is compact, and so each such finite union is compact: clearly it is still bounded, and a finite union of closed sets is still closed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Example 6 . Any finite alphabet is η -finite , with each symbol in a separate η -component. The subspace [ -2 , 1] ∪{ 2 } ⊆ R is η -finite. The subspace ( -2 , 1) ∪{ 2 } is not η -finite, since it is not compact. The subspace { 0 , 2 -n : n ∈ N } is compact but not η -finite, since it is not a finite union of path-connected sets. ■
texts.text: Example 6 . Any finite alphabet is η -finite , with each symbol in a separate η -component. The subspace [ -2 , 1] ∪{ 2 } ⊆ R is η -finite. The subspace ( -2 , 1) ∪{ 2 } is not η -finite, since it is not compact. The subspace { 0 , 2 -n : n ∈ N } is compact but not η -finite, since it is not a finite union of path-connected sets. ■

texts.label: text

texts.text: Both compactness and path-connectedness are preserved by continuous mappings and (finite) Cartesian products, see Facts A.2.4, A.2.5, A.2.7, and A.2.8. This gives us the corresponding results for η -finite spaces.

texts.text: Lemma 18. Continuous image of an η -finite space is η -finite.

texts.label: text

texts.text: Proof. Write X = ⋃ N i =1 X i for path-connected, compact sets X i . Let f : X → Y be continuous. We have:

texts.text: By Facts A.2.4 and A.2.7, each f ( X i ) is compact and path-connected. Thus by definition f ( X ) is η -finite.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 19. The Cartesian product X × Y space of η -finite spaces is η -finite. The η -components of X × Y are the products of η -components of X and η -components of Y .
texts.text: Lemma 19. The Cartesian product X × Y space of η -finite spaces is η -finite. The η -components of X × Y are the products of η -components of X and η -components of Y .

texts.orig: By Facts A.2.8 and A.2.5 each X i × Y j is path-connected. Therefore by def. X × Y is η -finite. Moreover, the η -components of X × Y are unions of the products X i × Y j . Now, fix i ∈ [1 ..n ] , j ∈ [1 ..j ] . Let Z be the η -componentof X × Y containing X i × Y j . consider the projection map π X : X × Y → X . As the projection is continuous, the image, π X ( Z ) is path-connected in X by Fact A.2.7. Moreover, X i ∈ π X ( Z ) . Thus, as X i is a maximal path-connected subspace of X , we have X i = π X ( Z ) . Similarly, considering the projection π Y : X × Y → X , we have Y j = π X ( Z ) . Since X i × Y j ⊆ Z , we therefore must have X i × Y j = Z . Therefore X × Y has finitely many η -components, and they are the products of η -components of X and η -components of Y .
texts.text: By Facts A.2.8 and A.2.5 each X i × Y j is path-connected. Therefore by def. X × Y is η -finite. Moreover, the η -components of X × Y are unions of the products X i × Y j . Now, fix i ∈ [1 ..n ] , j ∈ [1 ..j ] . Let Z be the η -componentof X × Y containing X i × Y j . consider the projection map π X : X × Y → X . As the projection is continuous, the image, π X ( Z ) is path-connected in X by Fact A.2.7. Moreover, X i ∈ π X ( Z ) . Thus, as X i is a maximal path-connected subspace of X , we have X i = π X ( Z ) . Similarly, considering the projection π Y : X × Y → X , we have Y j = π X ( Z ) . Since X i × Y j ⊆ Z , we therefore must have X i × Y j = Z . Therefore X × Y has finitely many η -components, and they are the products of η -components of X and η -components of Y .

texts.orig: Proof. It is sufficient to show this in the case that X has two η -components, say X 1 , X 2 . Define f : X 1 × X 2 → R ≥ 0 by f ( x 1 , x 2 ) = ∥ x 1 -x 2 ∥ . This is continuous, and so Im f is compact, as X 1 × X 2 is compact. Since X 1 , X 2 are disjoint, 0 / ∈ Im f . Thus 0 is not a limit point of Im f , and so for some δ > 0 we have that [0 , δ ) ⊈ Im f .

This is continuous, and so Im f is compact, as X 1 × X 2 is compact. Since X 1 , X 2 are disjoint, 0 / ∈ Im f . Thus 0 is not a limit point of Im f , and so for some δ > 0 we have that [0 , δ ) ⊈ Im f .

texts.orig: Lemma 22. Let X be an η -finite space and Σ a finite alphabet. Then a function f : X → Σ is continuous if and only if it is constant on the η -components of X
texts.text: Lemma 22. Let X be an η -finite space and Σ a finite alphabet. Then a function f : X → Σ is continuous if and only if it is constant on the η -components of X

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. ( ⇐ ) Suppose f : X → Σ is constant on η -components of X . Let ( x n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ X converge to x ∈ X . Then by Lemma 20, ( x n ) n ≥ 1 is eventually contained in the same η -component as x . Thus f ( x n ) = f ( x ) eventually, in particular f ( x n ) → f ( x ) as n →∞ . Hence f is continuous.
texts.text: Proof. ( ⇐ ) Suppose f : X → Σ is constant on η -components of X . Let ( x n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ X converge to x ∈ X . Then by Lemma 20, ( x n ) n ≥ 1 is eventually contained in the same η -component as x . Thus f ( x n ) = f ( x ) eventually, in particular f ( x n ) → f ( x ) as n →∞ . Hence f is continuous.

texts.label: text

texts.orig: Overall, the notions of η -finiteness and η -component have very favourable theoretical properties . Any continuous mapping f : X → Y , with X and Y η -finite, is guaranteed to map every element of an η -component of X into a single η -component of Y .
texts.text: Overall, the notions of η -finiteness and η -component have very favourable theoretical properties . Any continuous mapping f : X → Y , with X and Y η -finite, is guaranteed to map every element of an η -component of X into a single η -component of Y .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the case of η -finite systems, this means that the dynamics function acts on the η -components of the state-space (referred to as η -states) in the same way for each input within an η -component of the input-space (referred to as η -input). Moreover, every point within an η -component of the output function image (which is always η -finite), must be decoded as the same alphabet symbol. We formalize these properties in the following section.
texts.text: In the case of η -finite systems, this means that the dynamics function acts on the η -components of the state-space (referred to as η -states) in the same way for each input within an η -component of the input-space (referred to as η -input). texts.orig: B.4 Algebraic Theory of η -Finite Systems
texts.text: B.4 Algebraic Theory of η -Finite Systems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The connection between η -finite systems and canonical automata is extremely useful. It gives us a way to employ the powerful characterisations and results of AAT to any η -finite system dynamics. Namely, we can extend the notion of realisability to continuous η -finite systems, via the canonical automaton.
texts.text: The connection between η -finite systems and canonical automata is extremely useful. It gives us a way to employ the powerful characterisations and results of AAT to any η -finite system dynamics. Namely, we can extend the notion of realisability to continuous η -finite systems, via the canonical automaton.

texts.text: B.5 Cascade Decomposition and η -Finite Systems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section we bridge the gap between the AAT decomposition results, which apply to serial cascading, and our η -finite framework, which focuses on feed-forward connections. We begin by showing how taking the canonical semiautomaton 'commutes' with feed-forward cascading.
texts.text: In this section we bridge the gap between the AAT decomposition results, which apply to serial cascading, and our η -finite framework, which focuses on feed-forward connections. We begin by showing how taking the canonical semiautomaton 'commutes' with feed-forward cascading.

texts.orig: Altogether, we have a recipe for proving positive results. It is sufficient to show that an architecture can realise FLIP-FLOP, to show that it can implement all group-free functions with serial cascades. If it further can realize R 2 , then it can implement all group-free functions with feed-forward cascades.
texts.text: Altogether, we have a recipe for proving positive results. It is sufficient to show that an architecture can realise FLIP-FLOP, to show that it can implement all group-free functions with serial cascades. If it further can realize R 2 , then it can implement all group-free functions with feed-forward cascades.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 35. Suppose that η -finite dynamics D is a realisation of FLIP-FLOP , and η -finite dynamics E a realization of R 2 . Then feed-forward cascades of D and E components can implement all group free functions.
texts.text: Theorem 35. Suppose that η -finite dynamics D is a realisation of FLIP-FLOP , and η -finite dynamics E a realization of R 2 . Then feed-forward cascades of D and E components can implement all group free functions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let F be a group-free function. By Theorem 12, F is implemented by a serial cascade of FLIP-FLOP's, say C . By the construction in Appendix G.2, we have that C is realised by a feed-forward cascade of FLIP-FLOP's and repeat semiautomata, say C ′ . By Lemma 34, each repeat semiautomaton is a feed-forward cascade of R 2 components. Therefore C ′ is realised by a feed-forward cascade of FLIP-FLOP's components and R 2 components, say C ′′ . By Lemma 33, a feed-forward cascade of D and E components realises C ′′ , say C ′′′ . Thus, by transitivity of realisability, C ′′′ realises C , and thus by Theorem 31, C ′′ can implement F .
texts.text: Proof. Let F be a group-free function. By Theorem 12, F is implemented by a serial cascade of FLIP-FLOP's, say C . By the construction in Appendix G.2, we have that C is realised by a feed-forward cascade of FLIP-FLOP's and repeat semiautomata, say C ′ . By Lemma 34, each repeat semiautomaton is a feed-forward cascade of R 2 components. Therefore C ′ is realised by a feed-forward cascade of FLIP-FLOP's components and R 2 components, say C ′′ . By Lemma 33, a feed-forward cascade of D and E components realises C ′′ , say C ′′′ . Thus, by transitivity of realisability, C ′′′ realises C , and thus by Theorem 31, C ′′ can implement F .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this appendix we introduce a central notion of robustness that allows us to extend Metric Automata Theory to the study of concrete finite-precision implementations.
texts.text: In this appendix we introduce a central notion of robustness that allows us to extend Metric Automata Theory to the study of concrete finite-precision implementations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Arithmetic operations with floating point numbers are difficult to analyse, since addition and multiplication are not exactly commutative, associative and distributive. Thus, for example, the recurrent form and the convolutional form of the SSM update are not exactly equivalent (also noted by Merrill et al. [2024]-see footnote 3 in Definition 2.1). A theoretical framework which specifies an explicit datatype either is hard to analyse, or introduces additional simplifying assumptions.
texts.text: Arithmetic operations with floating point numbers are difficult to analyse, since addition and multiplication are not exactly commutative, associative and distributive. Thus, for example, the recurrent form and the convolutional form of the SSM update are not exactly equivalent (also noted by Merrill et al. [2024]-see footnote 3 in Definition 2.1). A theoretical framework which specifies an explicit datatype either is hard to analyse, or introduces additional simplifying assumptions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The central notion that allows us to extend Metric Automata Theory to the study of finite-precision implementations is the notion of ϵ -robustness . Intuitively, it describes stability of the dynamics under transition perturbations.
texts.text: The central notion that allows us to extend Metric Automata Theory to the study of finite-precision implementations is the notion of ϵ -robustness . Intuitively, it describes stability of the dynamics under transition perturbations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section C.1 we prove Theorem 2, thus showing that robustness provides a way to connect η -finite systems to their floating-point implementations on real-world computer architectures, without requiring us to commit to any particular standard of floating-point operations.
texts.text: In Section C.1 we prove Theorem 2, thus showing that robustness provides a way to connect η -finite systems to their floating-point implementations on real-world computer architectures, without requiring us to commit to any particular standard of floating-point operations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section C.2 we show that robustness provides stability under perturbing the parametrs of a model which describes the dynamics. We will later present a strongly robust dynamics based on the sLSTM model, which uses a particular choice of parameters. Our results show, that in such cases the parameters may be perturbed by some amount and the robust system will retain its behaviour.
texts.text: In Section C.2 we show that robustness provides stability under perturbing the parametrs of a model which describes the dynamics. We will later present a strongly robust dynamics based on the sLSTM model, which uses a particular choice of parameters. Our results show, that in such cases the parameters may be perturbed by some amount and the robust system will retain its behaviour.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lastly, in Section C.3 we prove Theorem 5 and further describe what kind of connecting functions are required for strongly robust η -finite cascades, by showing that 2-layer MLPs suffice.
texts.text: Lastly, in Section C.3 we prove Theorem 5 and further describe what kind of connecting functions are required for strongly robust η -finite cascades, by showing that 2-layer MLPs suffice.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Robustness marks the departure of Metric Automata Theory from Classical Automata and Formal Languages Theory, allowing us to study phenomena that do not occur with discrete state-spaces.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2. For ϵ > 0 and X ⊆ Ω X , U ⊆ Ω U , dynamics D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ are ϵ -robust (in Ω X ) if, for every x ∈ X and every u ∈ U , it holds that B Ω X ( f ( x, u ) , ϵ ) ⊆ X -i.e., y ∈ X for all y ∈ Ω X s.t. ∥ f ( x, u ) -y ∥ ≤ ϵ . Furthermore, we say that dynamics D are strongly ϵ -robust (in Ω X and Ω U ) if they are ϵ -robust (in Ω X ), each η -component of X contains an Ω X -ball of radius at least ϵ and each η -component of U contains an Ω U -ball of radius at least ϵ .
texts.text: Definition 2. For ϵ > 0 and X ⊆ Ω X , U ⊆ Ω U , dynamics D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ are ϵ -robust (in Ω X ) if, for every x ∈ X and every u ∈ U , it holds that B Ω X ( f ( x, u ) , ϵ ) ⊆ X -i.e., y ∈ X for all y ∈ Ω X s.t. ∥ f ( x, u ) -y ∥ ≤ ϵ . Furthermore, we say that dynamics D are strongly ϵ -robust (in Ω X and Ω U ) if they are ϵ -robust (in Ω X ), each η -component of X contains an Ω X -ball of radius at least ϵ and each η -component of U contains an Ω U -ball of radius at least ϵ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that the property of robustness is with respect to the ambient space Ω X , which contains the state space X . Thus, it is possible that a dynamics is ϵ -robust w.r.t. some ambient space (e.g., R ), and not ϵ -robust w.r.t. another ambient space (e.g., C ). This captures the property, that for a η -finite dynamics, a function approximating f within ϵ , and taking values in Ω , will implement the same transitions.

texts.orig: C.2 Parametrised Systems
texts.text: C.2 Parametrised Systems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The stability of robust dynamics can also be a desirable property in the context of learning. Consider a parametrised model describing the trained model. If the system described by the model is ϵ -robust and it is sufficiently smooth with respect to its parameters, then perturbing the model parameters will not change the behaviour of the system. Thus a robust system is intuitively more likely to be attained by a learning algorithm.
texts.text: The stability of robust dynamics can also be a desirable property in the context of learning. Consider a parametrised model describing the trained model. If the system described by the model is ϵ -robust and it is sufficiently smooth with respect to its parameters, then perturbing the model parameters will not change the behaviour of the system. Thus a robust system is intuitively more likely to be attained by a learning algorithm.

texts.text: Theorem 41. (Corollary 36.20 of [Willard, 2012]) A continuous functions on a compact metric space X is uniformly continuous , that is for all ϵ > 0 there exists δ > 0 such that for all x, y ∈ X || x -y || ≤ δ = ⇒ || f ( x ) -f ( y ) || ≤ ϵ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 42. Let η -finite dynamics D θ = ⟨ X,U,f θ ⟩ be parametrised by Θ , and let Θ be compact. Suppose D θ is ϵ -robust (w.r.t Ω X ). Then for some δ > 0 , we have that for ρ ∈ Θ s.t. || θ -ρ || ≤ δ the dynamics D ρ = ⟨ X,U,f ρ ⟩ is well-defined. Moreover, for any system S θ with dynamics D θ , the system S ρ obtained by switching out D θ for D ρ has the same canonical automaton.

Moreover, for any system S θ with dynamics D θ , the system S ρ obtained by switching out D θ for D ρ has the same canonical automaton.

texts.orig: Theorem 43. Let D be a strongly robust η -finite dynamics, which are a realisation of FLIP-FLOP . Then all group-free functions can be implemented by some strongly robust serial cascade of D components. Moreover, the connection functions in such cascade can be given by depth-2 MLPs.
texts.text: Theorem 43. Let D be a strongly robust η -finite dynamics, which are a realisation of FLIP-FLOP . Then all group-free functions can be implemented by some strongly robust serial cascade of D components. Moreover, the connection functions in such cascade can be given by depth-2 MLPs.

texts.text: Proof. We have that there are strongly robust xLSTM dynamics that realise FLIP-FLOP and R 2 . texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this Appendix we reap rewards of establishing the preliminary framework of Metric Automata Theory for η -finite dynamics. We can now prove expressivity results by establishing structural properties of dynamics, which are preserved by feed-forward cascades, and which are generally applicable.
texts.text: In this Appendix we reap rewards of establishing the preliminary framework of Metric Automata Theory for η -finite dynamics. We can now prove expressivity results by establishing structural properties of dynamics, which are preserved by feed-forward cascades, and which are generally applicable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section D.1 we introduce the notion of contracting dynamics, which describes dynamics that are not able to keep track of a state over unbounded input lengths. We use this notion to prove Theorems 3 and 4.
texts.text: In Section D.1 we introduce the notion of contracting dynamics, which describes dynamics that are not able to keep track of a state over unbounded input lengths. We use this notion to prove Theorems 3 and 4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section D.2 we introduce another structural property, called aperiodicity . It is the η -finiteness corresponding notion to group-freeness in Finite Automata. We use aperiodicity to prove Theorem 6.
texts.text: In Section D.2 we introduce another structural property, called aperiodicity . It is the η -finiteness corresponding notion to group-freeness in Finite Automata. We use aperiodicity to prove Theorem 6.

texts.text: D.1 Contracting Dynamics and Proofs of Theorems 3 and 4

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 30. Call η -finite dynamics ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ a contracting dynamics , if for any initial points x 0 , x ′ 0 ∈ X and eventually equivalent input sequences ( u n ) n ≥ 1 , ( u ′ n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ U , we have that the corresponding state sequences ( x n ) n ≥ 1 , ( x ′ n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ U are eventually equivalent.
texts.text: Definition 30. Call η -finite dynamics ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ a contracting dynamics , if for any initial points x 0 , x ′ 0 ∈ X and eventually equivalent input sequences ( u n ) n ≥ 1 , ( u ′ n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ U , we have that the corresponding state sequences ( x n ) n ≥ 1 , ( x ′ n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ U are eventually equivalent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thus, a for a contracting dynamics, it does not matter what state the evaluation of the inputs starts from-eventually all initial states lead to the same behaviour under a fixed input sequence. The intuition behind the name is the following-eventually all possible states that the dynamics could be in under the input sequence collapse to a single η -component.

texts.orig: Theorem 3 (Non-robustness of LRDs) . Suppose an η -finite LRD D is such that its canonical semiautomaton D A has at least two states, and an input inducing an identity transformation. Then D cannot be ϵ -robust for any ϵ > 0 .
texts.text: Theorem 3 (Non-robustness of LRDs) . Suppose an η -finite LRD D is such that its canonical semiautomaton D A has at least two states, and an input inducing an identity transformation. Then D cannot be ϵ -robust for any ϵ > 0 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ be an η -finite LRD, such that its canonical semiautomaton D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ has at least two distinct η -states, say x, x ′ , and an input u inducing identity transformation on X .
texts.text: Proof. Let D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ be an η -finite LRD, such that its canonical semiautomaton D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ has at least two distinct η -states, say x, x ′ , and an input u inducing identity transformation on X .

texts.orig: For contradiction suppose that D is robust. Then by Lemma 45, D is contracting. Thus for x 0 ∈ x, x ′ 0 ∈ x ′ and u ∈ u we have that the sequences ( x n = D ( x 0 , u n ) ) n ≥ 1 , ( x ′ n = D ( x ′ 0 , u n ) ) n ≥ 1 ∈ X ω are eventually equivalent. Since [ u ] ∼ U = u induces the identity transformation of X , we have that the corresponding sequences ([ x n ] ∼ X ) n ≥ 1 , ([ x ′ n ] ∼ X ) n ≥ 1 ∈ X ω are constant, equal x, x ′ respectively. Thus necessarily x = x ′ . This is a contradiction.
texts.text: For contradiction suppose that D is robust. Then by Lemma 45, D is contracting. Thus for x 0 ∈ x, x ′ 0 ∈ x ′ and u ∈ u we have that the sequences ( x n = D ( x 0 , u n ) ) n ≥ 1 , ( x ′ n = D ( x ′ 0 , u n ) ) n ≥ 1 ∈ X ω are eventually equivalent. Since [ u ] ∼ U = u induces the identity transformation of X , we have that the corresponding sequences ([ x n ] ∼ X ) n ≥ 1 , ([ x ′ n ] ∼ X ) n ≥ 1 ∈ X ω are constant, equal x, x ′ respectively. Thus necessarily x = x ′ . This is a contradiction.

texts.orig: Definition 31. For a η -finite space X , we say a sequence ( x n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ X η -converges in X , if eventually all its terms lie in the same η -component of X .
texts.text: Definition 31. For a η -finite space X , we say a sequence ( x n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ X η -converges in X , if eventually all its terms lie in the same η -component of X .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If the sequence of states of a system η -converges, it means that the behaviour of that system is eventually the same.
texts.text: If the sequence of states of a system η -converges, it means that the behaviour of that system is eventually the same.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 32. Call a η -finite dynamics D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ aperiodic , if for all x 0 ∈ X and input sequences ( u n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ U η -convergent in U , we have that the corresponding state sequence ( x n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ X is η -convergent in X .
texts.text: Definition 32. Call a η -finite dynamics D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ aperiodic , if for all x 0 ∈ X and input sequences ( u n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ U η -convergent in U , we have that the corresponding state sequence ( x n ) n ≥ 1 ⊆ X is η -convergent in X .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An example of a aperiodic dynamics is given by the FLIP-FLOP dynamics. An input sequence that η -converges must eventually be constantly set or reset . In that case, the state is eventually high , low respectively.

texts.orig: Sarrof et al. [2024] show that any star-free language can be recognized by an SSM like Mamba (Gu et al. [2022]), using the Krohn and Rhodes Theorem from Algebraic Automata Theory. However, in their construction, they assume that gates of the form A ( u ) = 0 can be used, which is not the case for architectures utilizing strictly positive parametrization, like Mamba.
texts.text: Sarrof et al. [2024] show that any star-free language can be recognized by an SSM like Mamba (Gu et al. [2022]), using the Krohn and Rhodes Theorem from Algebraic Automata Theory. However, in their construction, they assume that gates of the form A ( u ) = 0 can be used, which is not the case for architectures utilizing strictly positive parametrization, like Mamba.

texts.orig: Proof. Mamba blocks are feed-forward cascades of LRDs of the type considered in Lemma 51 and convolution blocks (FCDs)-see Figure 6. Thus η -finite feed-forward cascades of Mamba blocks are contracting, and so by Lemma 46, cannot implement FLIP-FLOP.
texts.text: Proof. Mamba blocks are feed-forward cascades of LRDs of the type considered in Lemma 51 and convolution blocks (FCDs)-see Figure 6. Thus η -finite feed-forward cascades of Mamba blocks are contracting, and so by Lemma 46, cannot implement FLIP-FLOP.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: E Geometrically Constrained Systems
texts.text: E Geometrically Constrained Systems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this appendix, we depart the setting of η -finiteness, and explore geometrically-constrained systems (GCSs) . This setting allows for systems implementing functions beyond regular, but shares many properties with the η -finite setting. We develop the theory of GCS to explain empirical capabilities of Mamba, and to showcase the flexibility and generalizability of Metric Automata Theory.
texts.text: In this appendix, we depart the setting of η -finiteness, and explore geometrically-constrained systems (GCSs) . This setting allows for systems implementing functions beyond regular, but shares many properties with the η -finite setting. We develop the theory of GCS to explain empirical capabilities of Mamba, and to showcase the flexibility and generalizability of Metric Automata Theory.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section E.1 we develop a notion analogous to aperiodicity from Section D.2. We then prove Theorem 9.
texts.text: In Section E.1 we develop a notion analogous to aperiodicity from Section D.2. We then prove Theorem 9.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section E.2 we introduce a generalisation of η -finiteness, called weak η -finiteness . We use it to argue that the cascade decomposition results for η -finite dynamics still apply to dynamics with convex-covering state-spaces.
texts.text: In Section E.2 we introduce a generalisation of η -finiteness, called weak η -finiteness . We use it to argue that the cascade decomposition results for η -finite dynamics still apply to dynamics with convex-covering state-spaces.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section E.3 we show that η -finite dynamics are a special case of convex-constrained dynamics. Finally, we show a construction of a FLIP-FLOP using a Mamba convex-constrained SSM, and argue using weakly η -finiteness that Theorem 8 holds.
texts.text: In Section E.3 we show that η -finite dynamics are a special case of convex-constrained dynamics. Finally, we show a construction of a FLIP-FLOP using a Mamba convex-constrained SSM, and argue using weakly η -finiteness that Theorem 8 holds.

texts.orig: Definition 33. For Ω = R d or C d , we call C ⊆ Ω a convex-covering if C is a finite union of open, convex sets in Ω . We say that X ⊆ Ω is convex-covered by C if X ⊆ C .
texts.text: Definition 33. For Ω = R d or C d , we call C ⊆ Ω a convex-covering if C is a finite union of open, convex sets in Ω . We say that X ⊆ Ω is convex-covered by C if X ⊆ C .

texts.text: Note: any convex set in Ω = R d or C d is path-connected. Thus any convex-covering C has finitely many path-connected components.

texts.orig: Definition 34. Let Ω = R d or Ω = C d , and let C ⊆ Ω . We say that dynamics D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ are convex-covered by C if X is convex-covered by C . We define a system geometrically-constrained by C as a tuple S C = ⟨ X,U,f,C,x 0 , Y, h ⟩ , where its dynamics ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ is a dynamics convex-covered by C , x 0 ∈ X is the initial state, and h : C × U → Y is the continuous output function. ■

texts.text: Lemma 52. For a cascade D = D 1 ⇝ · · · ⇝ D n with D i convex-covered/convex-separated by C i we have that C is convex-covered/convex-separated by C = C 1 ×··· × C n

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Suppose D i is convex-covered by C i for i ∈ [1 ..n ] . First, C 1 ×··· × C n is indeed a convexcovering. A product of convex sets is convex, and so a product of finite unions of convex sets is also a finite union of convex sets (by commutativity of set product and union, see proof of Lemma 19). Thus, X 1 ×··· × X n ⊆ C and D is convex-covered by C .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: E.2 Weakly η -finite Dynamics
texts.text: E.2 Weakly η -finite Dynamics

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section we introduce the topological notion of connectedness , as well as the necessary results to establish the finite state properties of GCSs where the state-space coincides with the convex-covering.
texts.text: In this section we introduce the topological notion of connectedness , as well as the necessary results to establish the finite state properties of GCSs where the state-space coincides with the convex-covering.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 39. A topological space X is called disconnected , if there are disjoint non-empty sets H,K in X such that X = H ∪ K . Then X is called connected if it is not disconnected.
texts.text: Definition 39. A topological space X is called disconnected , if there are disjoint non-empty sets H,K in X such that X = H ∪ K . Then X is called connected if it is not disconnected.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Connectedness is, as it turns out, a generalization of path-connectedness.
texts.text: Connectedness is, as it turns out, a generalization of path-connectedness.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fact E.2.1. (Theorem 27.2, [Willard, 2012]) Every path-connected space is connected.
texts.text: Fact E.2.1. (Theorem 27.2, [Willard, 2012]) Every path-connected space is connected.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Similarly to compactness and path-connectedness, connectedness is preserved by continuous mappings and products.
texts.text: Similarly to compactness and path-connectedness, connectedness is preserved by continuous mappings and products.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fact E.2.2. (Theorem 26.2, [Willard, 2012]) The continuous image of a connected space is connected.
texts.text: Fact E.2.2. (Theorem 26.2, [Willard, 2012]) The continuous image of a connected space is connected.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fact E.2.3. (Theorem 26.10, [Willard, 2012]) A nonempty product space is connected iff each factor space is connected.
texts.text: Fact E.2.3. (Theorem 26.10, [Willard, 2012]) A nonempty product space is connected iff each factor space is connected.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Similarly to path-connectedness, connectedness induces an equivalence on the space.
texts.text: Similarly to path-connectedness, connectedness induces an equivalence on the space.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 40. For x ∈ X , define C x as the union of connected subspaces of X containing x . We call it the C-component at x . We write x ≈ X y when y ∈ C x .
texts.text: Definition 40. For x ∈ X , define C x as the union of connected subspaces of X containing x . We call it the C-component at x . We write x ≈ X y when y ∈ C x .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note, that in [Willard, 2012] C-components are simply referred to as components .
texts.text: Note, that in [Willard, 2012] C-components are simply referred to as components .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fact E.2.4. ≈ X is an equivalence relation, partitioning X into maximal (with respect to inclusion) connected subspaces of X . C x is the equivalence class of ≈ X containing x . See Theorem 26.7 and Definition 26.11 of [Willard, 2012] for details.
texts.text: Fact E.2.4. ≈ X is an equivalence relation, partitioning X into maximal (with respect to inclusion) connected subspaces of X . C x is the equivalence class of ≈ X containing x . See Theorem 26.7 and Definition 26.11 of [Willard, 2012] for details.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fact E.2.5. (Theorem 26.12, [Willard, 2012]) The C-components of X are closed in X .
texts.text: Fact E.2.5. (Theorem 26.12, [Willard, 2012]) The C-components of X are closed in X .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thus, we think of C-components as a partition of the space that is a coarsening of the path-connected components. For an example of a space that has one C-connected component and 2 path-connected components, see the topologist's sine curve (Example 27.3, [Willard, 2012]).
texts.text: Thus, we think of C-components as a partition of the space that is a coarsening of the path-connected components. For an example of a space that has one C-connected component and 2 path-connected components, see the topologist's sine curve (Example 27.3, [Willard, 2012]).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 41. We call a space X weakly η -finite , if it has finitely many C-components.
texts.text: Definition 41. We call a space X weakly η -finite , if it has finitely many C-components.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Example 10 . Any finite alphabet is weakly η -finite, with each symbol being in a separate Ccomponent. ■
texts.text: Example 10 . Any finite alphabet is weakly η -finite, with each symbol being in a separate Ccomponent. ■

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our goal now is to show that weakly η -finiteness enjoys the same favourable theoretical properties as η -finiteness.
texts.text: Our goal now is to show that weakly η -finiteness enjoys the same favourable theoretical properties as η -finiteness.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 56. A continuous image of a weakly η -finite space is weakly η -finite.
texts.text: Lemma 56. A continuous image of a weakly η -finite space is weakly η -finite.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let C 1 , . . . , C n be the C-components of X , and let f : X → Y be continuous. Each f ( C i ) is connected, and so Im f is a union of finiely many connected spaces f ( C 1 ) , . . . , f ( C n ) . Thus, the equivalence classes of ≈ Im f must be unions of these images. Thus ≈ Im f must have finitely many equivalence classes.
texts.text: Proof. Let C 1 , . . . , C n be the C-components of X , and let f : X → Y be continuous. Each f ( C i ) is connected, and so Im f is a union of finiely many connected spaces f ( C 1 ) , . . . , f ( C n ) . Thus, the equivalence classes of ≈ Im f must be unions of these images. Thus ≈ Im f must have finitely many equivalence classes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 57. The Cartesian product X × Y space of weakly η -finite spaces is weakly η -finite. The C-components of X × Y are the products of C-components of X and C-components of Y .
texts.text: Lemma 57. The Cartesian product X × Y space of weakly η -finite spaces is weakly η -finite. The C-components of X × Y are the products of C-components of X and C-components of Y .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let C 1 , . . . , C n and E 1 , . . . , E , be the C-components of X,Y respectively. We have X = ⋃ n i =1 C i , Y = ⋃ m j =1 and so
texts.text: Proof. Let C 1 , . . . , C n and E 1 , . . . , E , be the C-components of X,Y respectively. We have X = ⋃ n i =1 C i , Y = ⋃ m j =1 and so

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: X × Y = ( n ⋃ i =1 C i ) × ( m ⋃ j =1 E j ) = n ⋃ i =1 m ⋃ j =1 C i × E j

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By Fact E.2.2 each C i × E j is connected. Thus, the C-components of X × Y are unions of the products C i × E j . Now, fix i ∈ [1 ..n ] , j ∈ [1 ..j ] . Let Z be the C-component of X × Y containing C i × E j . consider the projection map π X : X × Y → X . As the projection is continuous, the image, π X ( Z ) is connected in X . Moreover, C i ∈ π X ( Z ) . Thus, as C i is a maximal connected subspace of X , we have C i = π X ( Z ) . Similarly, considering the projection π Y : X × Y → X , we have E j = π X ( Z ) . Since C i × E j ⊆ Z , we therefore must have C i × E j = Z . Therefore X × Y has finitely many C-components, and they are the products of C-components of X and C-components of Y .
texts.text: By Fact E.2.2 each C i × E j is connected. Thus, the C-components of X × Y are unions of the products C i × E j . Now, fix i ∈ [1 ..n ] , j ∈ [1 ..j ] . Let Z be the C-component of X × Y containing C i × E j . consider the projection map π X : X × Y → X . As the projection is continuous, the image, π X ( Z ) is connected in X . Moreover, C i ∈ π X ( Z ) . Thus, as C i is a maximal connected subspace of X , we have C i = π X ( Z ) . Similarly, considering the projection π Y : X × Y → X , we have E j = π X ( Z ) . Since C i × E j ⊆ Z , we therefore must have C i × E j = Z . Therefore X × Y has finitely many C-components, and they are the products of C-components of X and C-components of Y .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 58. Let X be weakly η -finite and Σ be a finite alphabet. Then f : X → Σ is continuous if and only if it is constant on the C-components of X .
texts.text: Lemma 58. Let X be weakly η -finite and Σ be a finite alphabet. Then f : X → Σ is continuous if and only if it is constant on the C-components of X .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. ( ⇒ ) Let f : X → Σ be continuous. Let C be a C-component of X . By Fact E.2.2, f ( C ) ⊆ Σ is connected, and so f ( C ) = { σ } for some σ ∈ Σ . I.e., f is constant on the C-components of X .
texts.text: Proof. ( ⇒ ) Let f : X → Σ be continuous. Let C be a C-component of X . By Fact E.2.2, f ( C ) ⊆ Σ is connected, and so f ( C ) = { σ } for some σ ∈ Σ . I.e., f is constant on the C-components of X .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ( ⇐ ) Let f : X → Σ be constant on the C-components. Let Y ⊆ Σ be closed. Then f -1 ( Y ) ⊆ X must be a union of finitely many C-components, since X is weakly η -finite. By Fact E.2.5, we have that each C-component is closed, and therefore also f -1 ( Y ) is closed, as a finite union of closed sets. Thus f is continuous.

By Fact E.2.5, we have that each C-component is closed, and therefore also f -1 ( Y ) is closed, as a finite union of closed sets. Thus f is continuous.

texts.orig: Theorem 59. A convex-covering C is weakly η -finite, with its C-components coinciding with its path-connected components.
texts.text: Theorem 59. A convex-covering C is weakly η -finite, with its C-components coinciding with its path-connected components.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let C 1 , . . . , C n be path-connected components of C . Each C i is a union of finitely many open (in R d ) convex sets, and so is also open. Let Z be a C-component of C . Then Z is a union of the path-connected components, and so Z is also open. An open, connected subspace of R d is pathconnected, see Corollary 27.6 of [Willard, 2012]. Thus Z must actually be one of the path-connected components.
texts.text: Proof. Let C 1 , . . . , C n be path-connected components of C . Each C i is a union of finitely many open (in R d ) convex sets, and so is also open. Let Z be a C-component of C . Then Z is a union of the path-connected components, and so Z is also open. An open, connected subspace of R d is pathconnected, see Corollary 27.6 of [Willard, 2012]. Thus Z must actually be one of the path-connected components.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 60. Let D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ be a geometrically-contrained system, convex-covered by C , with X = C . Then D is weakly η -finite, and the C-components of X are the path-connected components.
texts.text: Lemma 60. Let D = ⟨ X,U,f ⟩ be a geometrically-contrained system, convex-covered by C , with X = C . Then D is weakly η -finite, and the C-components of X are the path-connected components.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. C has finitely many path-connected components, and so it is weakly η -finite, since pathconnectedness implies connectedness. Now, each C-component of C is a union of the path-connected components, all of which are open in Ω = R d . Hence each C-component of C is open in Ω . By Corollary 27.6 of [Willard, 2012], C-components of C are therefore path-connected. Thus the path-connected components and C-connected components of C coincide.
texts.text: Proof. C has finitely many path-connected components, and so it is weakly η -finite, since pathconnectedness implies connectedness. Now, each C-component of C is a union of the path-connected components, all of which are open in Ω = R d . Hence each C-component of C is open in Ω . By Corollary 27.6 of [Willard, 2012], C-components of C are therefore path-connected. Thus the path-connected components and C-connected components of C coincide.

If C i ∩ X = ∅ , take h ′ ( c, u ) = dec ◦ h ( x, u ) for ( c, u ) ∈ C i × U , where x ∈ X i . This is well-defined: For all x, x ′ ∈ C i ∩ X , since C is a convex-separator of X , we have that x and x ′ are in the same path-connected component of X . Therefore necessarily dec ◦ h ( x, u ) = dec ◦ h ( x ′ , u ) .

texts.orig: Theorem 8. SSMs with Mamba parametrisation can recognise all star-free languages as GCSs.
texts.text: Theorem 8. SSMs with Mamba parametrisation can recognise all star-free languages as GCSs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. By Construction 5, there is a Mamba block dynamics D , with a convex-covering state space, and η -finite input space, that realise FLIP-FLOP as weakly η -finite dynamics. A Mamba block can also have a convolution, and so there is a Mamba block dynamics E , with a convex-covering state space, and η -finite input space, that realise R 2 as weakly η -finite dynamics (details omitted. Also a sLSTM-like η -finite construction is possible, see Appendix G.3). Thus, by weakly η -finite analogue of Theorem 14, all group-free functions can be realized by feed-forward cascades of D and E components. Such cascades are actually constrained cascades of Mamba block GCSs, since the convex-coverings of D and E coincide with their state-spaces.
texts.text: Proof. By Construction 5, there is a Mamba block dynamics D , with a convex-covering state space, and η -finite input space, that realise FLIP-FLOP as weakly η -finite dynamics. A Mamba block can also have a convolution, and so there is a Mamba block dynamics E , with a convex-covering state space, and η -finite input space, that realise R 2 as weakly η -finite dynamics (details omitted. Also a sLSTM-like η -finite construction is possible, see Appendix G.3). Thus, by weakly η -finite analogue of Theorem 14, all group-free functions can be realized by feed-forward cascades of D and E components. Such cascades are actually constrained cascades of Mamba block GCSs, since the convex-coverings of D and E coincide with their state-spaces.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: F Details of The Experiments
texts.text: F Details of The Experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have created visualizations based on the [Liu et al., 2023] FLIP-FLOP task. The dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/synthseq/flipflop/ . The objective of the task is to predictively model a sequence of instructions of the form sx , where s ∈ w,r,i , x ∈ 0,1 . w indicates that the next symbol is to be stored, r indicates that the next symbol should be the retrieved value and i indicates no action. The specific task we trained on corresponds to the "clean" prediction mode, where only prediction following an r instruction need to be predicted. We note that the aim of our experiments was to obtain empirical evidence of Mamba having contracting dynamics, and a comprehensive experimental study is beyond the scope of our paper.
texts.text: We have created visualizations based on the [Liu et al., 2023] FLIP-FLOP task. The dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/synthseq/flipflop/ . The objective of the task is to predictively model a sequence of instructions of the form sx , where s ∈ w,r,i , x ∈ 0,1 . w indicates that the next symbol is to be stored, r indicates that the next symbol should be the retrieved value and i indicates no action. The specific task we trained on corresponds to the "clean" prediction mode, where only prediction following an r instruction need to be predicted. We note that the aim of our experiments was to obtain empirical evidence of Mamba having contracting dynamics, and a comprehensive experimental study is beyond the scope of our paper.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We trained 1-layer Mamba on sequence lengths 32, 64, and 512, observing similar state-collapse phenomena, as predicted by our results. Additionally [Sarrof et al., 2024] note that in their experiments Mamba needed more training steps to converge than reported by Liu et al. [2023] for an LSTM. This is another evidence towards the influence of robustness on stability of training.
texts.text: We trained 1-layer Mamba on sequence lengths 32, 64, and 512, observing similar state-collapse phenomena, as predicted by our results. Additionally [Sarrof et al., 2024] note that in their experiments Mamba needed more training steps to converge than reported by Liu et al. [2023] for an LSTM. This is another evidence towards the influence of robustness on stability of training.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: G.3.1 Strongly robust sLTSM FLIP-FLOP realization
texts.text: G.3.1 Strongly robust sLTSM FLIP-FLOP realization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We present a construction for a one layer sLSTM FLIP-FLOP, which is strongly robust. The key idea is to only use the h state to implement the dynamics. Then, we can use Theorem 42, and similar arguments involving uniform continuity, to extend the construction to be strongly robust in the states h, c, n and the input space u . We shall present the arguments in more detail here, to demonstrate how robustness can be used to prove properties of systems, in particular how to extend robustness to strong robustness.
texts.text: We present a construction for a one layer sLSTM FLIP-FLOP, which is strongly robust. The key idea is to only use the h state to implement the dynamics. Then, we can use Theorem 42, and similar arguments involving uniform continuity, to extend the construction to be strongly robust in the states h, c, n and the input space u . We shall present the arguments in more detail here, to demonstrate how robustness can be used to prove properties of systems, in particular how to extend robustness to strong robustness.
